coredata.dc:title: Study on self-healing and corrosion resistance behaviors of functionalized carbon dot-intercalated graphene-based waterborne epoxy coating
coredata.dc:creator: Ye, Yuwei
Chen, Hao
Zou, Yangjun
Zhao, Haichao
coredata.dc:description: Functionalized carbon dots (CDs) obtained from citric acid derivative were selected as intercalator to modify graphene and then dispersed into epoxy matrix to prepare CDs modified graphene/epoxy (CDs-G/EP) coatings. Meanwhile, their microstructure, self-healing and corrosion resistance behaviors were analyzed deeply. Structural characterizations indicated the formation of “π-π” interaction between functionalized carbon dots and graphene. By observation, the dispersion and interface compatibility of graphene were greatly enhanced by CDs. The change rules of electrochemistry results implied that the addition of 0.5 wt.% CDs-G in EP coating (CDs-G0.5 %/EP) demonstrated a superior protective property on steel, which was attributed to the physical barrier of highly dispersed graphene and the self-healing ability of CDs. After 50 days immersion, the oxygen permeability coefficient and water absorption of CDs-G0.5 %/EP coating were only 4.27 × 10−13 cm3 cm cm−2 s−1 Pa−1 and 4.4 %, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Functionalized carbon dots
Corrosion
Graphene
Self-healing

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure spectra of pristine and modified graphene: (a) FTIR; (b) UV–vis; (c) Raman; (d) XPS; (e) XPS-C 1s-CDs-G; (f) XPS-N 1s-CDs-G.
Morphology and dispersion of pristine and modified graphene: (a) SEM-G; (b) SEM-CDs-G; (c) EDS-O; (d) EDS-N; (e) TEM-G; (f) TEM-CDs-G; (g) TEM-G/EP; (h) TEM-CDs-G/EP.

Internal morphology of coating and the overall distribution of graphene in coating: (a) EP; (b) G0.5 %/EP; (d) CDs-G0.5 %/EP; (e) CDs-G1%/EP; Raman intensity maps for (c) G0.5 %/EP and (f) CDs-G0.5 %/EP.
OCP value in the steady state of coating.
Electrochemical impedance of coating in NaCl solution without and with the addition of graphene: (a, b) EP; (c, d) G0.5 %/EP; (e, f) CDs-G0.5 %/EP; (g, h) CDs-G1%/EP.
Equivalent circuit model fitted with the simple R(QR) (a) and R(QR(QR)) (b) electrical models.
Fitting parameters of coating during immersion process: (a) R c; (b) R ct; (c) Q c; (d) Q dl.
Self-healing behavior of coating in NaCl solution through SVET method: (a, b) EP; (c, d) G0.5 %/EP; (e, f) CDs-G0.5 %/EP; (g, h) CDs-G1%/EP.
Oxygen permeability coefficient (a) and water absorption (b) of coating.
Roughness of corrosion region on steel surface under different coatings: (a) EP; (b) G0.5 %/EP; (c) CDs-G0.5 %/EP; (d) CDs-G1%/EP.
SEM images and EDS spectra of corrosion region on steel surface under different coatings: (a, e-i) EP; (b, j) G0.5 %/EP; (c, k) CDs-G0.5 %/EP; (d, l) CDs-G1%/EP.
XPS-N 1s spectrum of steel surface under CDs-G0.5 %/EP coating after immersion test.
Corrosion protective mechanism diagram of all coatings: (a) EP; (b) G0.5 %/EP; (c) CDs-G0.5 %/EP; (d) CDs-G1%/EP.

head.ce:title: Study on self-healing and corrosion resistance behaviors of functionalized carbon dot-intercalated graphene-based waterborne epoxy coating

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry
Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Functionalized carbon dots (CDs) obtained from citric acid derivative were selected as intercalator to modify graphene and then dispersed into epoxy matrix to prepare CDs modified graphene/epoxy (CDs-G/EP) coatings. Meanwhile, their microstructure, self-healing and corrosion resistance behaviors were analyzed deeply. Structural characterizations indicated the formation of “π-π” interaction between functionalized carbon dots and graphene. By observation, the dispersion and interface compatibility of graphene were greatly enhanced by CDs. The change rules of electrochemistry results implied that the addition of 0.5 wt.% CDs-G in EP coating (CDs-G0.5 %/EP) demonstrated a superior protective property on steel, which was attributed to the physical barrier of highly dispersed graphene and the self-healing ability of CDs. After 50 days immersion, the oxygen permeability coefficient and water absorption of CDs-G0.5 %/EP coating were only 4.27 × 10−13 cm3 cm cm−2 s−1 Pa−1 and 4.4 %, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is the thinnest two-dimensional carbon material and the parent of all carbon materials [1–3]. The stack of graphene constitutes graphite, and the curl of graphene constitutes carbon nanotubes and fullerenes. Compared with other materials, graphene has higher mechanical strength, electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity, specific surface area and impermeability [4–6]. Graphene has been a research hotspot since the discovery of graphene, and its huge industrial development prospect has attracted widespread attention. Many researchers are committed to the theoretical research and application of graphene and its derivative materials in the fields of energy, electronics, biology, medicine, etc [7–9]. In the field of metal corrosion protection, graphene has also been extensively and deeply explored [10–12]. It mainly uses its excellent impermeability, modifiability and conductivity.

In general, graphene has two main application forms in the field of metal corrosion protection, which contains graphene anticorrosive film and graphene anticorrosive coating. Unfortunately, it is impossible to avoid the generation of defects in the graphene preparation process, which makes graphene unable to provide effective corrosion protection against defects such as wrinkles and cracks [13]. In this case, the corrosion of metal is significantly aggravated due to the micro-galvanic corrosion [14]. The long-term corrosion protection of metal materials still needs to rely on graphene anticorrosive coating. However, graphene is easy to agglomerate in polymer matrix due to its high specific surface area and interlayer force, which would accelerate the corrosion at exposed graphene/metal connections [15–17]. Therefore, it is an effective measure to improve the long-term protection of graphene anticorrosive coating by solving the dispersion of graphene in polymer matrix and endowing it a certain functionality [18–20].

Liu et al. [21] loaded sodium polyacrylate active agent on the surface of graphene through electrostatic adsorption, and found that the combination of graphene and active agent showed excellent dispersion and interfacial compatibility in ultrapure water and aqueous epoxy coatings, which was conductive to enhancing the anticorrosive ability. Based on the "π-π" interaction between poly 2-butylaniline and graphene as well as ultrasonic exfoliation, Chen et al. [22] significantly improved the dispersion of graphene in epoxy resins and appended it a certain self-healing ability. Ye et al. [23] used silanized oligoaniline to intercalate graphene to achieve effective dispersion in water-based epoxy resin and endowed it a certain electrical activity. Du et al. [24] fixed (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) -polystyrenesulfonic acid (PEDOT/PSS) in the interlayer structure of hydrotalcite and electrostatically assembled with graphene to obtain a smart graphene-based polyvinyl butyral coating. PEDOT/PSS not only could be adsorbed on the metal to exert corrosion inhibition function, but also could repair coating defects caused by ionic cross-linking. Javidparvar et al. [25–27] designed some graphene-based nanocontainers loaded with corrosion inhibitors and studied their corrosion resistance in epoxy coatings. The barrier properties of graphene and the self-healing properties of corrosion inhibitors could effectively enhance the anticorrosion ability of epoxy coating. Ramezanzadeh et al. [28–33] used Tamarindus indiaca extract, zinc doped-conductive polypyrrole, polyaniline/zinc cations, polydopamine-Zn, montmorillonite, polyaniline and benzimidazole-zinc phosphate to modify graphene or graphene oxide, and then endowed a certain self-healing ability to composite coating to prolong the service lifetime. Zhu et al. [34] pointed out that CDs could act as a dopant to give polymer coatings some self-healing properties. Ramezanzadeh et al. [35] revealed that the polyaniline tailored graphene oxide quantum dot could greatly improve the corrosion resistance of epoxy coating.

In this work, functionalized carbon dots obtained from citric acid derivative were chosen as intercalator to disperse graphene and then endowed it a certain self-healing ability. The relationship between carbon dots, graphene and corrosion protection ability of as-obtained epoxy composite coatings in 3.5 wt.% NaCl solution was systematically studied. The main objective was to develop a kind of self-healing graphene-based epoxy coating with good dispersion and barrier ability by optimizing CDs-G content, and then revealed the protection mechanism of coating in NaCl solution.

Graphene, ammonium citrate and ethyl alcohol were obtained from Taitan technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Epoxy resin and waterborne curing agent (polyamide) were provided from Changgan Chemical Co., Ltd. (Hangzhou, China). Q235 carbon steel electrodes were supplied from Xinyu steel company (Xinyu, China). Before electrochemical measurement, Q235 electrode was first polished with 400–1500 mesh emery papers, and then washed with ultrapure water and ethanol.

The preparation of functionalized carbon dots was shown in our previous work [36]. 0.25 wt.% functionalized carbon dots (25 mg) were mixed with 0.5 wt.% graphene (50 mg) and dispersed in ultrapure water by sonication. Then the dispersion was gradually added into epoxy resin (10 g) with stirring at 1200 r min−1 for 60 min. Subsequently, a waterborne curing agent of polyamide was added to the mixture solution and stirred at 1200 r min−1 for 20 min. Using the final solution as the precursor, the graphene/epoxy coating with thickness of 30 ± 2 μm was prepared on electrode surface by coating method, named as CDs-G0.5 %/EP specimen. In the meantime, the pure epoxy (EP) coating with 0.5 wt.% graphene (50 mg), 0. 25 wt.% functionalized carbon dots (25 mg) and 1 wt.% graphene (100 mg) were called as G0.5 %/EP and CDs-G1%/EP specimens, respectively.

The functional group of CDs-G was characterized by using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR), Ultraviolet spectrometer (UV–vis), X-ray photoelectronic spectroscopy (XPS) and Raman spectrometer. The morphology and dispersion of CDs-G in epoxy coating were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Before SEM observation, the coating was immersed into liquid nitrogen for 2 min to increase the brittleness and reduce the toughness, and then made it brittle fracture under mechanical action to obtain the ideal cross-section morphology. After that, the processed coating sample was cut into 70 nm thick slices with a microtome, then transferred to a copper mesh and dried for TEM observation. CHI-660E electrochemical workstation with traditional three-electrode system was used for electrochemical analysis, which was consisted of a working electrode (coated Q235 steel with an exposed area of 1 cm2), a platinum plate electrode and a saturated calomel electrode. In the frequency range of 105–10−2 Hz, a sinusoidal potential disturbance of 20 mV was applied for electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements. The self-healing performance of coating was evaluated by scanning vibrating electrode technology (SVET). Before test, the artificial defect was fabricated on the coating surface through a sharp knife and the defect size was about 0.1−0.3 cm2. The oxygen permeability coefficient of coating was analyzed by coating permeability testing machine. After corrosion, SEM was chosen to observe the 2D corrosion morphology, laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) was selected for the analysis of 3D surface morphology and XPS was used to analyze the absorption film on steel surface.

The structure spectra of pristine and modified graphene were shown in Fig. 1 . As seen in FTIR result, the peaks related to oxygen functional group at 1211.6 and 3402.3 cm−1 were observed on pristine graphene, which was closely related to its preparation method (Fig. 1(a)). However, some new peaks at 1641.9, 1715.8, 1307.2, 1389.3, 1615.3 and 2998.8 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CN, CO, CN, COO
−, NH and CH , respectively, indicating the successful modification of CDs [37]. From UV–vis result, the number of peak increased from 1 to 2 after CDs modification, and the peak at 348 nm was ascribed to n-π* electronic transition of CN bonds (Fig. 1(b)) [38]. Compared with previous work, the peak was red-shifted from 345 to 348 nm, implying the “π-π” interaction between functionalized carbon dots and graphene [36,39]. For Raman result, two obvious peaks at 1360.1 and 1600.8 cm−1 were detected, which belonged to the D and G bands, deriving from the vibration absorption of sp3 and sp2 carbon atoms on graphene skeleton (Fig. 1(c)). Meanwhile, these two peaks were red-shifted after modified by CDs, implying the interaction between CDs and graphene [40]. The I D /I G value can be used to reflect the degree of defect in graphene. The higher the value is, the more defect the graphene is. By modification, the I D /I G value was increased from 0.48 to 0.92, indicating the increase of graphene surface defect. This was because the insertion of CDs disrupted the state of the pristine graphene surface. From XPS result, three elements of C, N, and O were detected on modified graphene (Fig. 1(d)). Among them, N element came from functionalized carbon dots. Through fitting, the C 1s spectrum showed a binding energy value of 284.8, 286.2, and 288.2 eV, which was assigned to the CC/CC, CO/CN, and CO
, respectively (Fig. 1(e)) [41]. The N 1s spectrum could be decomposed to three peaks, including pyridine-like N, pyrrole-like N and graphitic N at 398.2, 399.5 and 401.1 eV, respectively (Fig. 1(f)) [42].

The morphology and dispersion of pristine and modified graphene were shown in Fig. 2 . The pristine graphene presented an obvious agglomeration in ultrapure water, while the structure of modified graphene is relatively flat (Fig. 2(a) and (b)). Through EDS analysis, the graphene was covered by evenly distributed O and N elements, demonstrating the uniform distribution of CDs (Fig. 2(c) and (d)). Form TEM result, the dispersion of pristine graphene in ultrapure water was poor (Fig. 2(e)). After adding CDs, the dispersion of modified graphene was sharply enhanced (Fig. 2(f)). In epoxy matrix, the modified graphene revealed a better interface compatibility compared with pristine graphene (Fig. 2(g) and (h)). Therefore, the modification of CDs greatly improved the dispersion and interface compatibility of graphene.

The internal morphology of coating and the overall distribution of graphene in coating were shown in Fig. 3 . A lot of micropores were observed in pure EP coating, presenting a loose structure (Fig. 3(a)). After adding proper pristine graphene, the micropore still existed in G0.5 %/EP coating although the number of micropores was greatly reduced, which was due to the low dispersion of pristine graphene (Fig. 3(b)). Differently, the CDs-G0.5 %/EP coating presented a very dense structure and no obvious micropore was found, explaining that the highly dispersed graphene filled the micropores and then enhanced the compactness of coating (Fig. 3(d)). With the increase of graphene content, the compactness of coating structure showed a downtrend (Fig. 3(e)). The intensity ratio of I epoxy/I G can indirectly reflect the overall distribution of graphene in coating. By Raman map scanning analysis, the I epoxy/I G ratio in G0.5 %/EP coating presented a great fluctuation (Fig. 3(c)). After adding CDs, the fluctuation of I epoxy/I G ratio was decreased. This symbolized the improved dispersion of graphene in EP coating, which conformed to the SEM and TEM results (Fig. 3(f)).

The OCP value in the steady state was calculated in Fig. 4 . The OCP value of pure EP coating after immersion test decreased from 0.11 V at 1 day to −0.40 V at 50 days. After the addition of graphene, different degrees of enhancement were observed on OCP value. Moreover, the addition of CDs played a positive effect on the enhancement of OCP value and the graphene content also affected the OCP value of coating. For example, the OCP value of CDs-G0.5 %/EP coating was about from 0.2 to -0.02 V during immersion, which was higher than that of G0.5 %/EP coating at the same condition. However, the OCP value showed the opposite trend as the graphene content continued to increase.

The electrochemical impedance of coating was obtained on the basis of OCP steady state in NaCl solution. Fig. 5 showed the effect of graphene content on the anticorrosive ability of coating. As seen in Nyquist images (Fig. 5(a), (c), (e) and (g)), the radius of capacitive reactance arc increased with the addition of graphene, manifesting that graphene could form a certain barrier to aggressive substance [43–45]. After the addition of CDs-G, the radius of capacitive reactance arc showed an uptrend. This was because the highly dispersed graphene fully exerted its barrier properties. In the meantime, the compactness of coating also played an important role to suppress the aggressive substance. With the graphene content increased to 1 wt.%, the radius of capacitive reactance arc began to rebound, which was attributed to the reduction of graphene dispersion and coating compactness. Furthermore, the impedance modulus of EP and G0.5 %/EP coatings decreased with the increase of immersion time (Fig. 5(b) and (d)). The reason was that the corrosive media would penetrate through the defects in the coating, and corrode the steel surface as the immersion time increased. Nevertheless, an abnormal phenomenon was observed on CDs-G0.5 %/EP and CDs-G1%/EP coatings. At a certain stage of the immersion process, the impedance modulus of these coatings was suddenly higher than the previous moment, which was mainly due to the self-healing ability of CDs (Fig. 5(f) and (h)).

The equivalent circuit model was fitted by simple R(QR) and R(QR(QR)) models during the immersion process and the results were shown in Fig. 6 . Among them, the R s was the solution resistance, the R c denoted the coating resistance, the R ct was the charge transfer resistance, the Q c and Q dl were the phase components of the coating capacitance and the electric double layer capacitance, respectively [46–48]. Fig. 6(a) and (b) represented the two stages of immersion process. The critical point of equivalent circuit model for EP, G0.5 %/EP, CDs-G0.5 %/EP and CDs-G1%/EP coatings was 5, 10, 20 and 20 days, respectively. The appearance of R ct and Q dl symbolized that the corrosion medium had reached the substrate surface. By statistics, the fitting parameters were presented in Fig. 7 . At the beginning of immersion process, the R c exhibited a downward trend and the Q c revealed an uptrend as Cl− diffused into the interface and corroded the steel surface (Fig. 7(a) and (c)). Nevertheless, the variation trend of coating was inhibited to some degrees after adding graphene. Due to the highest compactness, the CDs-G0.5 %/EP coating presented the highest coating resistance and the lowest coating capacitance during entire immersion stage. As seen in Fig. 7(b) and (d), the corrosion medium passed through the pure EP coating with many micropores after 5 days immersion. Due to the barrier effect of graphene, the time for corrosive medium to pass through the G0.5 %/EP coating was postponed to 10 days. Through CDs modification, the highly dispersed graphene could greatly fill the micropores in the coating, which increased the time for corrosive medium to reach the substrate until 20 days. After that, the corrosion reaction on the interface became stronger as the immersion experiment went on. Thus, the R ct was decreased and the Q dl was increased with time.

Fig. 8 revealed the self-healing behavior of coating with artificial defect in NaCl solution through SVET method. In the case of EP coating, the current density of local defect was almost positive on the test region and the value was higher compared with other coatings, implying the severe corrosion in the test region (Fig. 8(a) and (b)). After the addition of graphene (G0.5 %/EP coating), the current density of local defect manifested a significant reduction on test region, suggesting that the attack of corrosion ion was weaken to a certain degree (Fig. 8(c) and (d)). Meanwhile, the current density of local defect on EP and G0.5 %/EP coatings was increased after 24 h immersion, explaining that the corrosion was deteriorated. However, the current density of local defect on CDs-G0.5 %/EP coating was the lowest among all coatings, indicating the strongest protective effect (Fig. 8(e) and (f)). The graphene content also played an important role in the protective effect. By observation, the current density of local defect on CDs-G1%/EP coating displayed an increase trend with the increase of graphene content, proving that the agglomeration of graphene would availably induce the corrosion (Fig. 8(g) and (h)). Besides, the current density of local defect on CDs-G0.5 %/EP and CDs-G1%/EP coatings was decreased after 24 h immersion, revealing the self-healing ability.

The oxygen permeability coefficient (P O2) and water absorption of coating were shown in Fig. 9 . By measurement, the P O2 value of EP coating was the highest (2.03 × 10−12 cm3 cm cm−2 s−1 Pa−1), which was due to its poor compactness (Fig. 9(a)). After the addition of graphene, the P O2 value exhibited a downtrend. For G0.5 %/EP coating, the P O2 value was about 4.27 × 10−13 cm3 cm cm−2 s−1 Pa−1, which was one order of magnitude lower than EP coating. Through CDs modification, the P O2 value of CDs-G0.5 %/EP coating was reduced by two orders of magnitude compared with EP coating. However, excessive graphene would aggravate the generation of defects, resulting in an increase in the P O2 value. The water absorption of coating was calculated by BK equation in our previous studies [12,23]. Due to the penetration of corrosive media, the water absorption of coating increased as the immersion experiment went on (Fig. 9(b)). After 50 days immersion, the water absorption of EP coating was up to 7.5 %, which was higher than other coatings. The addition of graphene and the modification of CDs effectively reduced the water absorption of EP coating. When 0.25 wt.% CDs and 0.5 wt.% graphene were added into EP coating, the water absorption was the lowest.

The LCSM and SEM images were used to analyze and characterize the steel surface under different coatings after corrosion in NaCl solution. The roughness of steel surface under different coatings after corrosion was shown in Fig. 10 . Through 50 days immersion, it was obvious that the steel surface was very rough. By measurement, the roughness of steel surface under EP coating protection was about 2.904 μm, which was the highest among all specimens, and this phenomenon was consistent with the analysis of corrosion morphology (Fig. 10(a)). The roughness of steel surface was reduced to 1.524 μm after protected by G0.5 %/EP coating (Fig. 10(b)). Through CDs modification, the dispersion of graphene was sharply enhanced, resulting in the lowest value of steel surface roughness under CDs-G0.5 %/EP coating protection (Fig. 10(c)). Nevertheless, the agglomeration caused by excessive graphene in CDs-G1%/EP coating would increase the roughness of steel surface (Fig. 10(d)).

SEM images and EDS spectra of corrosion region on steel surface under different coatings were shown in Fig. 11 . The surface of steel under EP coating protection was destroyed by corrosion (Fig. 11(a)). Through EDS scanning analysis, a large number of Fe, O, and Cl elements were distributed on the surface, implying serious corrosion (Fig. 11(e)–(i)). The SEM images under graphene-based composite protection coatings showed that the corrosion of steel surface was alleviated by the barrier ability of graphene (Fig. 11(b)–(d)). In the meantime, the intensity of Cl in EDS spectra was suppressed after adding CDs-G, implying the reduction of corrosion (Fig. 11(j)–(l)). Among all coatings, the steel surface under CDs-G0.5 %/EP coating protection was the smoothest although the invasion of corrosive media. By XPS analysis, the N 1s spectrum of steel surface under CDs-G0.5 %/EP coating protection showed three peaks at 398.8, 400.8 and 401.5 eV, which corresponded to NC, NFe and N +H, respectively (Fig. 12 ) [49–51]. This finding showed that the CDs were successfully adsorbed on the steel surface.

The corrosion protective mechanism diagram of all coatings was shown in Fig. 13 . Due to the common defects of EP coating, the oxygen and corrosive media could easily penetrate the EP coating to reach the surface of metal substrate, causing serious corrosion (Fig. 13(a)). Once the local corrosion was formed, it would break the original balance and promote the occurrence of corrosion. Although the addition of graphene could hinder the penetration of corrosive media, the graphene was easy to agglomerate in EP coating due to the high specific surface area and interlayer force (Fig. 13(b)). Thus, the barrier ability of agglomerate graphene in G0.5 %/EP coating was limited. By CDs modification, the graphene not only filled the defects to enhance the compactness of EP coating, but also was evenly distributed in EP coating (Fig. 13(c)). This behavior could make full use of the barrier properties of graphene. Meanwhile, the CDs could be well adsorbed on the exposed steel surface through the coordination bond between heteroatom and steel substrate, which played a certain self-healing ability. With the increase of graphene content, excessive graphene would agglomerate in the coating, leading to a reduction in shielding performance (Fig. 13(d)).

In this work, a kind of functionalized carbon dots modified graphene-based epoxy composite coating was synthesized on polished steel substrates by coating method, and its microstructure, self-healing ability, and corrosion behavior were analyzed. The structure characterization showed that the graphene achieved high-efficiency dispersion in ultrapure water and excellent interfacial compatibility in epoxy resin through the modification of functionalized carbon dots. The electrochemical results showed that the well-dispersed graphene could effectively improve the barrier properties of epoxy coating and endow it a certain self-healing properties. After adding 0.5 wt.% CDs-G, the CDs-G0.5 %/EP coating showed the best corrosion resistance. After 50 days immersion, the impedance modulus of CDs-G0.5 %/EP coating was enhanced by two orders of magnitude, and the oxygen permeability coefficient as well as water absorption were greatly lowered compared with pure epoxy coating.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Scientific Research Foundation of Jiangxi University of Science and Technology (No. 205200100421), the Jiangxi Provincial Department of Education (No. GJJ190469), Postdoctoral fund of Jiangxi Province (2019KY29), the Research and Development Project of Ganzhou, the Science and Technology Innovation Talents Program of Ganzhou, and the Program of Qingjiang Excellent Young Talents (Jiangxi University of Science and Technology).
